coredata.dc:title: Rapid microwave-assisted synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yaling
Zheng, Jingxia
Wang, Junli
Yang, Yongzhen
Liu, Xuguang
coredata.dc:description: Highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized rapidly by one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using citric acid as carbon source and ethylenediamine as dopant. The influences of reaction temperature, reaction time and raw material ratio on the fluorescence performance of N-CDs were investigated. Then N-CDs with the highest quantum yield were selected as fluorescent materials for fabricating white light-emitting diodes (LEDs). Highly luminescent N-CDs with the quantum yield of 75.96% and blue-to-red spectral composition of 51.48% were obtained at the conditions of 180 °C, 8 min and the molar ratio of citric acid to ethylenediamine 2:1. As-prepared highly luminescent N-CDs have an average size of 6.06 nm, possess extensive oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on their surface, and exhibit strong absorption in ultraviolet region. White LEDs based on the highly luminescent N-CDs emit warm white light with color coordinates of (0.42, 0.40) and correlated color temperature of 3416 K.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave-assisted hydrothermal method
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
White light-emitting diodes
Optical property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and analysis of orthogonal experiment
Process optimization and structural characterization of highly luminescent N-CDs
Process optimization of highly luminescent N-CDs
Further structural characterization of highly luminescent N-CDs
Application of highly luminescent N-CDs in white LEDs
Feasibility of highly luminescent N-CDs as phosphor for white LEDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesizing N-CDs and their application in white LEDs.
QY measurement of N-CDs under different reaction conditions.
Trend analysis of orthogonal test results.
Photographs of the resulting samples under daylight (a), the dilute sample solution under daylight (b) and UV light (c, λex = 365 nm); From left to right: N-CDs-r (r = 1, 2, …, 9).
HRTEM images of N-CDs-r (r = 1, 2, …, 9 for a, b, …, i, respectively).
Size distribution histograms of N-CDs-r (r = 1, 2, …, 9 for a, b, …, i, respectively).

(a–c) FT-IR spectra corresponding to different temperatures at a fixed nCA:nEDA (2:1 (a), 2:3 (b) and 2:5 (c)); (d–f) FT-IR spectra corresponding to the different nCA:nEDA at a fixed reaction temperature (150 °C (d), 180 °C (e) and 210 °C (f)).
XRD pattern (inset: SAED pattern) of highly luminescent N-CDs.
XPS full scan (a), high resolution C1s (b), high resolution N1s (c) and FT-IR spectra (d) of highly luminescent N-CDs.
UV–vis absorption, PLE (a) and PL spectra (b) of highly luminescent N-CDs, the inset of (b) shows photos of N-free-CDs (left) and highly luminescent N-CDs (right) under UV light (λex = 365 nm).
Visualized surface states of N-CDs with excitation-dependent and -independent behaviors.
Spectral composition of highly luminescent N-CDs under 360 nm excitation.
PL spectra (a), relative fluorescence intensity and optimal emission peak position (b) of highly luminescent N-CDs under different UV irradiation times.
Emission spectrum (a) and CIE chromaticity diagram (b) of white LEDs based on highly luminescent N-CDs, inserts are the emission spectrum of UV chip (a) and the photo of white LEDs under 3.2 V.
Emission spectra (inset: relative radiance) of the white LEDs based on highly luminescent N-CDs at different working time intervals.
Levels and factors affecting the QY of N-CDs.
Orthogonal test arrangement and QY value to optimize synthesis parameter of highly luminescent N-CDs. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: CA (2.1014 g, 0.01 mol), deionized water (15 mL).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The number of carbon is set to be 1, the number of hydrogen, nitrogen and oxygen is a, b and c, respectively.

head.ce:title: Rapid microwave-assisted synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for white light-emitting diodes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized rapidly by one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using citric acid as carbon source and ethylenediamine as dopant. The influences of reaction temperature, reaction time and raw material ratio on the fluorescence performance of N-CDs were investigated. Then N-CDs with the highest quantum yield were selected as fluorescent materials for fabricating white light-emitting diodes (LEDs). Highly luminescent N-CDs with the quantum yield of 75.96% and blue-to-red spectral composition of 51.48% were obtained at the conditions of 180 °C, 8 min and the molar ratio of citric acid to ethylenediamine 2:1. As-prepared highly luminescent N-CDs have an average size of 6.06 nm, possess extensive oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on their surface, and exhibit strong absorption in ultraviolet region. White LEDs based on the highly luminescent N-CDs emit warm white light with color coordinates of (0.42, 0.40) and correlated color temperature of 3416 K.

ce:section.ce:para: As a promising energy-saving technique for sustainable development, solid-state white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) have received widespread attention because of their high energy efficiency, low power consumption, long working life, rapid response time and environmental friendliness [1,2]. Currently, the commercial route for fabricating white LEDs is combining blue chip with yellow Y3Al5O12:Ce3+ (YAG:Ce3+) phosphor [3]. Nevertheless, these phosphors often suffer from expensive raw materials and high reaction temperature [4]. Most recently, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs)-based white LEDs have undergone rapid development owing to their favorable optical properties such as high quantum yield (QY), tunable emission and high photostability [5,6]. However, the preparation and separation process of those QDs often involve the inherent toxicity and underlying environmental contamination, substantially limiting their large-scale application [7].

Carbon dots (CDs), with an amorphous or sp2 carbon framework and a surface grafted with oxygen-containing groups, have been dramatically studied over the past decade owing to their admirable optical properties, robust chemical inertness and low toxicity [8,9]. To date, extensive methods have been reported for the synthesis of CDs, including arc discharge [10], laser ablation [11], electrochemical synthesis [12], ultrasonic treatment [13], pyrolysis [4], and hydrothermal synthesis [14], and so forth. Nonetheless, most of these methods are undesirable on account of time-consuming operation, harsh reaction conditions, tedious purification process and inferior yield [15]. Therefore, it remains challenging to develop a simple, rapid and efficient synthesis method. Recently, microwave-assisted synthesis has received increasing favor because of its outstanding merits, such as straightforward operation, high reaction speed, mild reaction condition, safety and no pollution [16,17]. However, at present, the QY of CDs obtained by microwave-assisted synthesis is relatively low (<40%) [15,17,18], greatly hindering their practical application in the photoelectric field.

In the last few years, various strategies, like surface passivation [19], surface reduction [20], metal coating [21] and internal doping [22], have been developed to improve QY of CDs. Among them, internal doping usually requires only one-step reaction. Besides, the introduction of heteroatoms and the tuning of internal/surface groups can be realized by changing the species of dopant, a preferred method adopted by researchers for improving QY of CDs. Xu et al. synthesized yellowish-green nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with QY of 10.1% by microwave irradiation from calcium citrate and urea [17]. Hu et al. developed a simple one-pot microwave-assisted approach for the large-scale preparation of CDs with QY of 14.3% [15]. Zhai et al. developed N-CDs with highest QY of 30.2% using citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and amines as N-doping precursors, including ethylenediamine (EDA), diethylamine, triethylamine and 1,4-butanediamine [18]. In order to preferably exert the role of CDs as a color converter and enhance the brightness and light efficiency of CDs-based LEDs in the field of lighting, the preparation of CDs with higher QY is still a major challenge at this stage.

Herein, we report a rapid and one-step microwave-assisted method to synthesize N-CDs with high QY using CA and EDA as starting materials (Fig. 1 ). The effects of reaction temperature, reaction time and raw materials ratio on QY of CDs were investigated by orthogonal experiment. Ultimately, warm white LEDs were fabricated by combining the highly luminescent N-CDs and UV-LED chip, thus promoting the research and application of CDs fluorescent materials in white LEDs lighting field.

CA and EDA were ordered from Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Quinine sulfate was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were used as received. Deionized water was used throughout the whole experiment process. Optical sealants (OE-6250A and OE-6250B) were supplied by Dow Corning Toray Co., Ltd (Tokyo, Japan). UV-LED chip (output power = 1.0 W) centered at 365 nm was supplied by Guanghuashi Technology Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and analysis of orthogonal experiment

ce:section.ce:para: In the present study, the effects of reaction temperature (factor A), reaction time (factor B) and the molar ratio of CA to EDA (nCA:nEDA, factor C) on QY of N-CDs were explored on the basis of an orthogonal experimental design and the corresponding levels and factors are tabulated as Table 1 . An orthogonal array L9(34) was applied to assign the considered factors and levels as indicated in Table 2 . Generally, each row of orthogonal array refers to a specific set of factor levels. A column could only be assigned to a factor. Nine trials according to the orthogonal array L9(34) were carried out to accomplish the optimization process, during which the prepared samples and measured QY value are denoted as N-CDs-r and Yr (r = 1, 2, …, 9), respectively. Note: Unless specified, the sample utilized for characterization or application in this paper referred to highly luminescent N-CDs obtained under optimal conditions. In particular, the order of the trials should be randomized to avoid any personal or subjective prejudice.

There are three critical parameters in the analysis of orthogonal experiment: Kji, kji and Rj. Among them, Kji refers to the sum of the evaluation indexes of all levels (i, i = 1, 2, 3) in each factor (j, j = A, B, C). The optimal level for each factor could be obtained when Kji is the largest. kji (average value of Kji) is usually used to determine the optimal level and the best combination of factors. Rj represents the range between the maximum and minimum value of kji, which is used to assess the significance of the factors, i.e. a larger Rj stands for a greater impact of the factor on the experimental index [23,24]. Specifically, the calculation of Kji, kji and Rj are shown below:
(1)
K
A 1
=
Y 1
+
Y 2
+
Y 3
K
B 1
=
Y 1
+
Y 4
+
Y 7
K
C 1
=
Y 1
+
Y 6
+
Y 8
K
A 2
=
Y 4
+
Y 5
+
Y 6
K
B 2
=
Y 2
+
Y 5
+
Y 8
K
C 2
=
Y 2
+
Y 4
+
Y 9
K
A 3
=
Y 7
+
Y 8
+
Y 9
K
B 3
=
Y 3
+
Y 6
+
Y 9
K
C 3
=
Y 3
+
Y 5
+
Y 7
(2)
k
j i
=
K
j i
/ 3
( i =
1 ,
2 ,
3 ;
j = A , B , C )
(3)
R j
= max
(
k
j i
)
− min
(
k
j i
)
( i =
1 ,
2 ,
3 ;
j = A , B , C )
where Yr is the QY value of the resulting N-CDs-r (r = 1, 2, 3, …, 9).

Fluorescent N-CDs were synthesized by one-step microwave-assisted method using CA as carbon source and EDA as dopant. Specifically, CA (2.1014 g, 0.01 mol), and a certain volume of EDA and deionized water (15 mL) were added into a 30 mL reaction vial and heated at 150–210 °C using microwave irradiation for 2–8 min in MICROWAVE 300 reactor. The pressure remained at about 10 bar during the process of the reaction. After the reaction, the resulting transparent solution was filtered with 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove larger particles. The N-CDs powder was then obtained by freeze drying. In addition, the EDA-free controlled trial (N-free-CDs) was conducted only using CA under the same experiment condition as highly luminescent N-CDs (180 °C, 8 min).

The highly luminescent N-CDs powder (28 mg) with high QY was mixed with silicone (4 mL, OE-6250A:OE-6250B = 1:1) and heated at 30 °C for 30 min with constant magnetic stirring. Then the highly luminescent N-CDs/silicone mixtures (30 μL) were gently dropped onto the commercial cupped optical lens and thermally cured in a drying oven at 80 °C for 8 h. At last, the optical lens was firmly fastened on the UV-LED chip with the emission peak wavelength of 365 nm to achieve the fabrication of white LEDs.

All the measurements were performed at room temperature. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern were obtained from a JEOL JEM-2010 microscope operated at accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was depicted using a Rigaku D/max-2500 diffractometer. Elemental analysis (EA) was performed by using a Vario EL cube analyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) observation was taken on a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer equipped with a monochromated Al Kα excitation source. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained from a BRUKER TENSOR27 spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a HITACHI U-3900 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were collected on a HORIBA Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 luminescence spectrometer using Xe lamp as the excitation source. The emission spectrum, color coordinates and correlated color temperature of the white LEDs were measured by PR655 Spectrascan spectrometer.

The QY of all N-CDs was measured according to the established procedure using quinine sulfate (QS) in 0.10 M H2SO4 solution as the standard (QY = 54%) [14,25]. All the absorbance values of the solutions (less than 0.1 at 360 nm to minimize the re-absorption effect) were collected on a UV–vis absorption spectrometer. Meanwhile, the corresponding PL spectra at 360 nm of all solutions were recorded on a fluorescence spectrometer. The integrated PL intensity is the area under the PL curve in the wavelength range 380–700 nm. A linear fitting was plotted using the integrated PL intensity against the absorbance with intercept at zero, during which the slope (K) and R-square (R2) were obtained. The closer the R2 is to 1, the better the linear relationship and the less the error. The QY is calculated using the below equation:
(4)
Q =
Q
s t
×
(
K x
/
K
s t
)
×
(
η x
/
η
s t
)
2
where Q is the QY of N-CDs, K is the slope of fitted line, η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript ‘‘st’’ and ‘‘x’’ stand for the standard solution and N-CDs, respectively. For the solutions in the current study, η/ηst ≈ 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Process optimization and structural characterization of highly luminescent N-CDs
Process optimization of highly luminescent N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To synthesize highly luminescent N-CDs from CA and EDA, a series of samples were prepared by designing three factors and three levels orthogonal experiments (Table 1). The corresponding reaction conditions and results are summarized in Table 2. QY of N-CDs is very susceptible to the reaction temperature, while it is less affected by the molar ratio and almost independent of the reaction time (Table 2, Figs. 2 and 3
).

In addition, we find that the content of carboxyl is effected directly by the nitrogen content under certain temperature condition. With the molar ratio of CA and EDA from 2:1 to 2:3, then to 2:5, the amount of carboxyl group rapidly decreases and then disappears (Fig. 7d–f), during which the intensity of amide (amide I and amide II band) gradually increases and QY slightly decreases (Fig. 3). These results further demonstrate that the carboxyl group of CA and amino group of EDA undergo a dehydration-condensation reaction [28]. Besides, moderate residual carboxyl group may be more conducive to the enhancement of fluorescence. A reasonable explanation is that the carboxyl group could introduce trapping states to boost CDs emit light [29].

From the above, when the reaction temperature is 180 °C, the reaction time is 8 min and the molar ratio of CA to EDA is 2:1, the QY of resulting N-CDs-6 is up to 75.96%. Therefore, the N-CDs-6 (i.e. the highly luminescent N-CDs mentioned below) was selected to analyze the following morphology, structure, optical properties and white LEDs application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Further structural characterization of highly luminescent N-CDs
Application of highly luminescent N-CDs in white LEDs
Feasibility of highly luminescent N-CDs as phosphor for white LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further confirm the feasibility of highly luminescent N-CDs as phosphor with the help of UV-LED chip to fabricate white LEDs, the spectral composition of highly luminescent N-CDs was analyzed by dividing the emission wavelength of 380–700 nm into violet (380–450 nm), blue (450–495 nm), green (495–570 nm), yellow (570–590 nm), orange (590–650 nm) and red (650–700 nm). As shown in Fig. 12 , the blue-to-red spectral composition of highly luminescent N-CDs is calculated to be 51.48%, which is higher than that the value reported by Kwon et al. [39]. This phenomenon may be attributed to the higher π-electron density on the surface of CDs. High-density π electrons can reduce the energy band gap of π-π* transition, and then reduce the energy band gap in the CDs surface state, consequently resulting in a redshift of the emission wavelength to increase the blue-to-red spectral composition of CDs [35]. This higher blue-to-red spectral composition contributes to the improved efficiency of converting UV light into white light, thus making the highly luminescent N-CDs suitable for phosphor application in white LEDs.

The PL stability of highly luminescent N-CDs solution upon UV exposure (power: 6W; wavelength: 365 nm) was also recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 13 , the optimal emission peak position of highly luminescent N-CDs is almost unchanged, while the fluorescence intensity constantly decreases with prolonging UV irradiation time. This result may be caused by the chemical structure of highly luminescent N-CDs, which mainly consists of amorphous carbon (Fig. 8). Moreover, the emission behavior induced by the surface state is susceptible to environmental factors. After continuous high power UV light irradiation, the fluorescent molecular group or auxochrome may be seriously oxidized and the degree of electron activity on the excited state is greatly reduced, ultimately leading to gradually weakened fluorescence intensity [27]. Fortunately, the PL stability to UV light is considerably higher for CDs in solid state or in nanocomposites than in solution. Accordingly, the highly luminescent N-CDs powder obtained by freeze drying is dispersed in silicone to improve its fluorescence stability and prevent PL quenching, realizing the practical application of highly luminescent N-CDs in the lighting field of white LEDs.

In view of the low toxicity, high QY and broad blue-to-red spectral composition of as-prepared highly luminescent N-CDs, white LEDs were fabricated with highly luminescent N-CDs/silicone as a color converter to transform UV light originated from a 365 nm UV-LED chip into white light effectively. The emission spectrum of this white LEDs (Fig. 14 a) takes on a notably weak peak from 380 to 420 nm, a weak peak at 442 nm and a strong peak centered at 572 nm. The former is derived from the very small amounts of UV leakage, indicating that most of UV light emitted from the UV-LED chip (inset in Fig. 14a) has been absorbed to produce white light. Beyond that, the peak centered at 442 and 572 nm originated from the highly luminescent N-CDs phosphor can almost extend the entire visible region (420–700 nm). Additionally, the top emission peak of the white LEDs shifts to longer wavelength compared with that of the highly luminescent N-CDs solution (Fig. 10b). This observation might be attributed to the energy transfer induced by the reabsorption of the highly luminescent N-CDs in the condensed matter [32,39]. Shorter wavelength light emitted from some N-CDs may be reabsorbed by surrounding N-CD particles, from which longer wavelength light is reemitted. After repeated reabsorption and reemission, short wavelength light is significantly reduced and long wavelength light is rapidly increased, which provides a persuasive explanation for the broad emission focused on 572 nm [34,39]. The as-obtained white LEDs exhibit bright warm white light (inset in Fig. 14b) with the color coordinates of (0.42, 0.40) and the correlated color temperature of 3416 K, which is adaptable for indoor illumination [8].

In addition, the stability of the white LEDs based on highly luminescent N-CDs at different working time intervals has been investigated (Fig. 15 ). It is found that the stability of highly luminescent N-CDs dispersed in silicone for the white LED is higher than that in solution (Fig. 13), which is beneficial to the application of highly luminescent N-CDs in white LED. The reason of this excellent stability is that the exciton in the excited singlet state is hard to annihilate in silicone [40]. In other words, the highly luminescent N-CDs are more inert and their movements are more blocked in silicone than in solution, thus decreasing the probability of collisions among the excitons in the highly luminescent N-CDs. The above results indicate that highly luminescent N-CDs phosphor has a potential application in the lighting field of white LEDs.

In summary, we synthesized highly luminescent N-CDs by a facile one-step microwave-assisted method from CA and EDA. The influence of reaction parameters (temperature, time and raw material ratio) on QY was investigated, among which the optimized N-CDs possess high QY of 75.96% and blue-to-red spectral composition of 51.48%. The as-obtained highly luminescent N-CDs display strong absorption in UV region and bright blue fluorescence under UV light illumination. Also, we exploited highly luminescent N-CDs/silicone phosphors as a light converter in combination with UV-LED chip to fabricate white LEDs. The eco-friendly white LEDs emit bright warm white light with the color coordinates of (0.42, 0.40) and the correlated color temperature of 3416 K, suggesting great potential application in the solid-state lighting systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1610255, U1607120), Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Technology (201605D131045 -10) and Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Program (201603D111010).

Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection

Freestanding luminescent films of nitrogen-rich carbon nanodots toward large-scale phosphor-based white-light-emitting devices
